one_line_takeaway: Zer0n hashes LLM (Gemini 2.0 Pro)-generated vulnerability reports and anchors the hashes on the Avalanche C-Chain to make post-hoc report tampering detectable, at a measured 22.9% workflow-time overhead and sub-100ms verification cost, while explicitly leaving blockchain-consensus and TEE compromise out of scope.
key_finding: On the 500-endpoint/100-contract dataset, Zer0n achieved precision 78%, recall 82%, F1-score 80% for vulnerability detection. Adding blockchain-backed integrity logging increased total workflow time from a baseline of 12.39s to 15.23s, a 22.9% overhead (analysis execution 12.34s -> 12.89s; report generation 45.2ms -> 48.7ms), dominated by network propagation (~2.18s) and block confirmation (~14.2s) on the Fuji testnet, which the paper notes does not block the analysis workflow since logging is asynchronous. Hash computation overhead was <1ms (0.82ms measured) and full verification completed in ~90ms (Table 7), with reported tamper-detection accuracy of 100.0% and false-positive rate of 0.0% for the integrity-check mechanism itself (distinct from the 78%/82%/80% vulnerability-detection metrics). In the single reentrancy case study, Slither flagged the pattern generically via state-mutability heuristics, while Zer0n (via Gemini 2.0 Pro) additionally identified the multi-step malicious-fallback attack vector and suggested a Checks-Effects-Interactions remediation.
core_contribution: Zer0n, a prototype hybrid-trust framework that pairs an LLM-driven (Gemini 2.0 Pro) vulnerability-analysis engine with blockchain-backed integrity logging on the Avalanche C-Chain. Analysis execution stays off-chain for speed; only a SHA-256 hash of each generated vulnerability report is written on-chain (via a minimal Solidity contract, logVulnerabilityHash) so post-analysis tampering of the report becomes cryptographically detectable. The system also uses the same Avalanche smart contracts to gate Basic/Pro subscription access. The paper's own contributions, as stated by the authors, are: (1) the hybrid trust architecture decoupling AI reasoning from integrity enforcement, (2) the tamper-evident Solidity logging mechanism, and (3) an empirical feasibility/overhead analysis of adding this blockchain layer to a security pipeline.
threat_model: Explicitly scoped in the paper (Table 3 / Section 6.1): the adversary is assumed capable of post-analysis report tampering and local artifact manipulation, both of which Zer0n is designed to detect via hash verification against the on-chain record. The Node.js execution environment and the Gemini API connection are explicitly trusted up to the point of hash generation. Out of scope: blockchain consensus compromise, denial-of-service on the ledger, compromise of the trusted execution environment, network-level attacks, and active exploitation of the vulnerabilities the system discovers. This is a narrow integrity/provenance threat model, not a threat model for the vulnerability-detection task itself.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: the evaluation is a feasibility study, not exhaustive benchmarking; it does not assess production-scale deployment or scalability; the framework does not perform automated remediation or exploit execution; the full AI analysis pipeline remains closed-source 'pending safety reviews,' limiting reproducibility (only a minimal Solidity/verification artifact subset was released, and the supplementary repo link is anonymized for review); and the threat model excludes consensus compromise, ledger DoS, and TEE compromise. Additional issues found in the text itself: the reentrancy 'ablation' comparison is a single qualitative sample, not a statistically powered study, so the LLM-vs-static-analysis claim is illustrative rather than general; Table 5 lists Zer0n as having 'Auto-Remediation: Yes (AI-Gen)' which appears to directly contradict the Discussion's statement that Zer0n 'does not address automated remediation'; and two cited references ([18] and [19]) carry placeholder-looking arXiv IDs (2405.00000 and 2409.00000), suggesting citation-list quality issues that a reader should independently verify before relying on them.

datasets_used: 100 smart-contract samples sourced from the SWC Registry and OWASP Juice Shop
quantitative_results: Baseline analysis execution time: 12.34 +/- 1.21 s vs Zer0n: 12.89 +/- 1.35 s
Baseline report generation: 45.2 +/- 8.1 ms vs Zer0n: 48.7 +/- 9.2 ms
Total workflow time: baseline 12.39 s vs Zer0n 15.23 s (22.9% overhead)
Transaction construction: 12.4 +/- 2.3 ms; transaction signing: 8.7 +/- 1.8 ms
Network propagation (Fuji testnet): 2,180 +/- 450 ms; block confirmation: 14,200 +/- 1,800 ms
Local hash recomputation: 0.79 +/- 0.12 ms; on-chain hash retrieval: 89.3 +/- 18.7 ms; total verification time: 90.1 +/- 18.9 ms
Tamper detection accuracy: 100.0%; false positive rate on integrity check: 0.0%
baselines_compared: Slither (static analysis, IR-based) - empirically run head-to-head on one reentrancy case study; also listed in feature/comparison tables (Table 1, Table 5)
Mythril (symbolic/concolic execution) - listed only in the literature comparison table (Table 1), not empirically run
ContractFuzzer, OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite - listed only in the qualitative feature comparison table (Table 5), not empirically run
AICyberChain (neural-network-based, IoT-data focus) - cited and placed in the comparison table (Table 1) as related prior work, not empirically run
